info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Matrices and Determinants

questiontext.text: Let \(A\) be \(m\times m\) and \(D\) be \(n\times n\) invertible matrices. Then \(\det\begin{pmatrix}A & B \\ C & D\end{pmatrix}\) equals:

feedback.text: Correct. By Schur complement: \(\det\begin{pmatrix}A&B\\C&D\end{pmatrix}=\det(D)\det(A-BD^{-1}C)\). This is the Schur complement formula.
Incorrect. This is true only when \(B=0\) or \(C=0\) (block triangular case).
Incorrect. There is no such simple subtraction formula in general.
Incorrect. This would be valid only for \(1\times1\) blocks (the \(2\times2\) determinant).

questiontext.text: If \(A\) and \(B\) are \(n\times n\) matrices with \(\det(A)=3\) and \(\det(B)=-2\), then \(\det(A^2B^T)\) equals:

feedback.text: Correct. \(\det(A^2B^T)=\det(A)^2\det(B^T)=9\cdot(-2)=-18\). (Using \(\det(B^T)=\det(B)\).)
Incorrect. \(\det(B)=-2\) so \(\det(A^2B^T)=9\times(-2)=-18\).
Incorrect. \(\det(A^2)=9\), not \(3+3=6\).
Incorrect. \(\det(A^2B^T) = 9\cdot(-2) = -18\).

questiontext.text: The \((i,j)\) cofactor of a matrix \(A\) is \(C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j}M_{ij}\) where \(M_{ij}\) is the \((i,j)\) minor. The inverse formula states:

answer.text: \(A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det A}\text{adj}(A)\) where \(\text{adj}(A)_{ij} = C_{ji}\) (transpose of cofactor matrix)

feedback.text: Correct. The adjugate (classical adjoint) is the transpose of the cofactor matrix, and \(A^{-1}=\text{adj}(A)/\det(A)\).
Incorrect. The adjugate is the TRANSPOSE of the cofactor matrix: \((adj\,A)_{ij}=C_{ji}\).
Incorrect. It is \(A^{-1} = \text{adj}(A)/\det(A)\), not multiplied by the determinant.
Incorrect. Division by \(\det(A)\) is required: \(A\cdot\text{adj}(A)=\det(A)\cdot I\).

questiontext.text: The Vandermonde determinant \(\det\begin{pmatrix}1&a&a^2\\1&b&b^2\\1&c&c^2\end{pmatrix}\) equals:

feedback.text: Correct. The Vandermonde determinant for nodes \(a,b,c\) is \(\prod_{i>j}(x_i-x_j)=(b-a)(c-a)(c-b)\).
Incorrect. The correct formula is \((b-a)(c-a)(c-b)\). The sign depends on the ordering convention.
Incorrect. The Vandermonde determinant factors as a product of differences of the parameters, not their product.
Incorrect. This is an expanded form that differs from the standard Vandermonde result by sign. The standard result is \((b-a)(c-a)(c-b)\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following is true about the trace of a matrix?

answer.text: \(\text{tr}(AB) = \text{tr}(BA)\) for any \(m\times n\) matrix \(A\) and \(n\times m\) matrix \(B\)

feedback.text: Correct. \(\text{tr}(AB)=\sum_{i,j}A_{ij}B_{ji}=\text{tr}(BA)\). This is the cyclic property of trace.
Incorrect. Trace is not multiplicative. For example, \(\text{tr}(I_n\cdot A)=\text{tr}(A)\neq n\cdot\text{tr}(A)\) in general.
Incorrect. Trace is linear: \(\text{tr}(A+B)=\text{tr}(A)+\text{tr}(B)\).
Incorrect. The trace of the inverse is not simply the reciprocal of the trace.

questiontext.text: For a matrix \(A\), the matrix exponential is \(e^A = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \frac{A^k}{k!}\). Which of the following holds?

feedback.text: Correct. If eigenvalues of \(A\) are \(\lambda_i\), eigenvalues of \(e^A\) are \(e^{\lambda_i}\), so \(\det(e^A)=\prod e^{\lambda_i}=e^{\sum\lambda_i}=e^{\text{tr}A}\).
Incorrect. \(e^Ae^B=e^{A+B}\) only when \(AB=BA\) (they commute). In general, the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff formula applies.
Incorrect. \(e^A\) is always invertible with \((e^A)^{-1}=e^{-A}\), regardless of \(A\).
Incorrect. The correct relation is \(\det(e^A)=e^{\text{tr}A}\), not \(\text{tr}(e^A)=e^{\det A}\).

questiontext.text: A matrix \(A\) is rank-deficient (singular) iff \(\det(A)=0\). Which statement follows directly?

answer.text: The homogeneous system \(Ax=0\) has a nontrivial solution iff \(\det(A)=0\)

feedback.text: Correct. \(Ax=0\) has a nontrivial solution iff the null space is nontrivial iff \(A\) is not invertible iff \(\det(A)=0\).
Incorrect. Even for singular \(A\), \(Ax=b\) may have solutions (infinitely many) if \(b\in\text{col}(A)\).
Incorrect. Many nonzero matrices have zero determinant (e.g., any rank-deficient matrix).

answer.text: All eigenvalues of \(A\) are zero if \(\det(A)=0\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\det(A)=0\) means zero is an eigenvalue, but other eigenvalues can be nonzero.

questiontext.text: The LU decomposition of a square matrix \(A\) (without pivoting) writes \(A = LU\) where \(L\) is lower triangular with 1s on the diagonal and \(U\) is upper triangular. Which is true?

feedback.text: Correct. Since \(\det(L)=1\) (all 1s on diagonal), \(\det(A)=\det(L)\det(U)=\det(U)=\prod u_{ii}\).

answer.text: Every matrix has an LU decomposition without pivoting

feedback.text: Incorrect. Some matrices require row permutation (partial pivoting) for LU decomposition, e.g., \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\).

answer.text: The LU decomposition is unique for every invertible matrix

feedback.text: Incorrect. The decomposition is unique only with the normalization that \(L\) has 1s on the diagonal; the \(U\) diagonal entries can be redistributed.
Incorrect. \(L\) and \(U\) are generally unrelated except for symmetric positive definite matrices (\(A=LDL^T\) Cholesky).

questiontext.text: Hadamard's inequality states: for an \(n\times n\) real matrix \(A\) with columns \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\), \(|\det(A)| \leq \prod_{j=1}^n \|a_j\|\). Equality holds iff:

feedback.text: Correct. Hadamard equality holds iff the columns form an orthogonal set. Geometrically, the volume of the parallelepiped is maximized when the edges are perpendicular.
Incorrect. Unit norm columns don't guarantee equality; orthogonality is the key condition.
Incorrect. \(\det=0\) gives equality trivially (both sides are 0 if some column is zero), but the meaningful equality condition is orthogonality with nonzero columns.
Incorrect. Symmetry does not imply equality in Hadamard's inequality.

questiontext.text: How does the determinant change under each elementary row operation?

answer.text: Row swap: \(\det\to-\det\); multiply row by \(c\): \(\det\to c\cdot\det\); add multiple of row to another: \(\det\) unchanged

feedback.text: Correct. These are the fundamental effects of elementary row operations on the determinant.

answer.text: Row swap: \(\det\to\det\); multiply row by \(c\): \(\det\to c\cdot\det\); add multiple of row: \(\det\to\det+c\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Row swaps negate the determinant; adding a multiple of one row to another does not change the determinant.
Incorrect. Row swaps negate \(\det\) and scaling multiplies \(\det\) by the scalar.
Incorrect. These operations change the sign or scale the determinant; they don't make it zero.

questiontext.text: Let \(D_n = \det(T_n)\) where \(T_n\) is the \(n\times n\) tridiagonal matrix with 2s on the diagonal and \(-1\)s on the super/sub-diagonals. The recurrence is \(D_n = 2D_{n-1} - D_{n-2}\), with \(D_1=2\), \(D_2=3\). What is \(D_n\)?

feedback.text: Correct. Solving the recurrence \(D_n = 2D_{n-1}-D_{n-2}\) with initial conditions \(D_1=2, D_2=3\) gives \(D_n=n+1\).
Incorrect. If \(D_n=2^n\), then \(D_1=2\), \(D_2=4\neq3\).
Incorrect. \(D_3=2\cdot3-2=4\) but \(3^2=9\neq4\).
Incorrect. \(D_1=1\neq2\) under this formula.

questiontext.text: Sylvester's law of inertia states: for any real symmetric matrix \(A\), the number of positive, negative, and zero eigenvalues (the signature) is preserved under congruence transformations \(A\mapsto P^TAP\) for invertible \(P\). For \(A = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\), the signature is:

feedback.text: Correct. Eigenvalues are \(+1\) and \(-1\): one positive, one negative, zero zero-eigenvalues. Signature is \((1,1,0)\).
Incorrect. The eigenvalue \(-1\) is negative.
Incorrect. The eigenvalue \(+1\) is positive.
Incorrect. Both eigenvalues \(\pm1\) are nonzero; the zero count is 0.

questiontext.text: Cramer's rule states: for \(Ax=b\) with invertible \(A\), the solution component \(x_i = \det(A_i)/\det(A)\) where \(A_i\) is \(A\) with its \(i\)-th column replaced by \(b\). For \(\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\5&3\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x_1\\x_2\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\end{pmatrix}\), \(x_1\) equals:

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\det(A_1)=1\cdot3-1\cdot2=1\), \(\det(A)=1\), so \(x_1=1\).
Incorrect. \(x_1=1\); it is \(x_2=-1\).
Incorrect. Cramer's rule gives \(x_1=\det(A_1)/\det(A)=1/1=1\).

questiontext.text: The spectral norm (induced 2-norm) of a matrix \(A\) is \(\|A\|_2 = \sigma_{\max}(A)\), the largest singular value. For a real symmetric positive definite matrix \(A\) with eigenvalues \(0 < \lambda_1\leq\cdots\leq\lambda_n\), \(\|A\|_2\) equals:

feedback.text: Correct. For symmetric PD matrices, singular values equal eigenvalues, so the spectral norm is the largest eigenvalue \(\lambda_n\).
Incorrect. Singular values of a symmetric PD matrix equal its eigenvalues (not square roots), so \(\|A\|_2=\lambda_n\).
Incorrect. The spectral norm is the largest eigenvalue, not the average eigenvalue.
Incorrect. \(\sqrt{\text{tr}(A^2)}=\|A\|_F\) is the Frobenius norm, not the spectral norm.

questiontext.text: A circulant matrix is determined by its first row \((c_0,c_1,\ldots,c_{n-1})\): each row is the previous row shifted one position right (cyclically). The eigenvalues of an \(n\times n\) circulant matrix are \(\lambda_k = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1}c_j\omega^{jk}\) for \(k=0,\ldots,n-1\), where \(\omega = e^{2\pi i/n}\). The eigenvectors are:

feedback.text: Correct. All circulant matrices of size \(n\) share the same eigenvectors: the columns of the DFT matrix.
Incorrect. Circulant matrices are diagonalized by the DFT matrix, not the identity.

answer.text: Arbitrary vectors depending on the specific circulant

feedback.text: Incorrect. All \(n\times n\) circulant matrices share the same eigenvectors (DFT basis).

answer.text: The rows of the circulant matrix itself

feedback.text: Incorrect. The eigenvectors are the DFT columns, not the rows of the circulant.

questiontext.text: The Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury identity states: for invertible \(A\) and matrices \(U,V\) of appropriate sizes, \((A+UCV)^{-1} = A^{-1} - A^{-1}U(C^{-1}+VA^{-1}U)^{-1}VA^{-1}\). As a special case (\(C=I, U=u, V=v^T\)), the rank-1 update formula \((A+uv^T)^{-1}\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The Sherman-Morrison formula: \((A+uv^T)^{-1}=A^{-1}-\frac{A^{-1}uv^TA^{-1}}{1+v^TA^{-1}u}\), valid when \(1+v^TA^{-1}u\neq0\).
Incorrect. This is missing the denominator \(1+v^TA^{-1}u\) and has the wrong sign.
Incorrect. The rank-1 perturbation is to \(A\), not to \(A^{-1}\); the formula differs.
Incorrect. The Sherman-Morrison formula has a scalar denominator, not a simple matrix factor.

questiontext.text: The \(k\)-th compound matrix \(C_k(A)\) of an \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\) has entries equal to the \(k\times k\) minors of \(A\). The size of \(C_k(A)\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. There are \(\binom{n}{k}\) ways to choose \(k\) rows and \(\binom{n}{k}\) ways to choose \(k\) columns, giving a \(\binom{n}{k}\times\binom{n}{k}\) matrix.
Incorrect. The size is \(\binom{n}{k}\times\binom{n}{k}\), not \(n^k\times n^k\).
Incorrect. Each entry of the compound matrix is a \(k\times k\) minor, but the compound matrix itself is \(\binom{n}{k}\times\binom{n}{k}\).
Incorrect. The compound matrix is larger than \(n\times n\) for most values of \(k\).

questiontext.text: For a rank-1 matrix \(A = uv^T\) (outer product of column vectors \(u\) and \(v\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), \(n\geq2\)), the determinant \(\det(I + uv^T)\) equals:

feedback.text: Correct. By the matrix determinant lemma: \(\det(I+uv^T)=1+v^Tu\). This follows from Sherman-Morrison or directly from the rank-1 update formula.
Incorrect. The determinant is \(1+v^Tu\), the inner product, not the product of norms.
Incorrect. \(u\) and \(v\) are vectors, not square matrices; their determinants are not defined.
Incorrect. The matrix determinant lemma gives \(\det(I+uv^T)=1+v^Tu\), not \(n+v^Tu\).

questiontext.text: For a \(2n\times2n\) skew-symmetric matrix \(A\) (\(A^T=-A\)), the Pfaffian \(\text{pf}(A)\) satisfies \(\det(A) = \text{pf}(A)^2\). For the \(2\times2\) matrix \(A=\begin{pmatrix}0&a\\-a&0\end{pmatrix}\):

feedback.text: Correct. \(\det\begin{pmatrix}0&a\\-a&0\end{pmatrix}=0\cdot0-a\cdot(-a)=a^2\). The Pfaffian of the \(2\times2\) skew-symmetric matrix is just \(a\).
Incorrect. \(\text{pf}(A)=a\) and \(\det(A)=a^2=\text{pf}(A)^2\).
Incorrect. \(\det\begin{pmatrix}0&a\\-a&0\end{pmatrix}=a^2\geq0\) (always non-negative for skew-symmetric).
Incorrect. Unless \(a=0\), the Pfaffian is \(a\neq0\).

questiontext.text: A Cauchy matrix has entries \(C_{ij} = \frac{1}{x_i+y_j}\) for distinct \(x_i\) and \(y_j\). Its determinant is:

feedback.text: Correct. The Cauchy determinant formula: the numerator is the product of differences among \(x_i\)s and among \(y_j\)s, and the denominator is the product of all \(x_i+y_j\).
Incorrect. This would be the product of all entries, not the determinant.
Incorrect. The Cauchy matrix is invertible (nonzero determinant) whenever the \(x_i\) are distinct and the \(y_j\) are distinct.
Incorrect. This is not the Cauchy determinant formula.
